key_insight: In this pilot biomonitoring study, prostate cancer survivors in New Jersey showed measurable PFAS exposure in both serum and tap water, with higher levels observed in non-White participants.
clinical_relevance: The findings suggest environmental exposure may be an important survivorship factor to consider in future prostate cancer research, especially when studying health disparities and treatment-related vulnerability.
method: Recruited 124 men with prostate cancer from Rutgers Cancer Institute (active surveillance or 3–12 months after definitive treatment).
Collected serum and residential tap water samples for PFAS testing using mass spectrometry and modified EPA Method 537.
Compared PFAS levels by race and assessed serum–tap water correlations.
evidence_signal: Among 235 eligible patients, 60% enrolled; six serum PFAS analytes were detected in >80% of samples, nine tap-water analytes in >60%, and serum–tap water correlations were modest (r=0.22–0.41).
watch_item: This is a small pilot study with incomplete sample collection, so results are exploratory and not yet enough to establish causal links or broad population-level risk.
paper_title: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances Exposure in New Jersey Prostate Cancer Survivors: A Pilot Biomonitoring Study
